title: AgentDose: Steroid Dose Extraction from OMOP
subtitle: NLP parsers + LLM agents for scalable medication extraction
problem: Corticosteroid exposure is a critical variable in pharmacoepidemiology and rheumatology research, but dosing information in EHR data is largely trapped in free-text prescription signatures — not in structured fields. This means most OMOP-based studies on steroid exposure are either impossible or rely on rough proxies that don't capture titration, tapering, or actual administered dose.
approach: AgentDose combines rule-based NLP parsers with LLM agents to extract structured dosing information (drug, dose, frequency, duration, route) from prescription sig fields in OMOP CDM databases. The hybrid approach uses deterministic parsers for well-formed sigs and LLM agents for ambiguous or complex cases, with a validation layer comparing against manually adjudicated gold standards.
impact: Nominated for Best Community Contribution Showcase at OHDSI Global Symposium 2025. The framework is being developed as an open-source R package to support reproducible steroid exposure calculation across distributed OHDSI partner databases, enabling multi-site pharmacoepidemiologic analyses that were previously infeasible.
myRole: Led development of the extraction framework, designed and evaluated NLP parsers and LLM-based approaches, presented at OHDSI 2025. Developing the open-source R package for multi-site deployment.

pipeline.label: OMOP Drug Records
pipeline.description: Source: OMOP CDM drug_exposure table, containing prescription records with sig (signature) free-text fields alongside structured fields.

pipeline.label: Rule-Based Parser
pipeline.description: A deterministic NLP parser extracts dose, frequency, route, and duration from well-formed prescription signatures using regex patterns and clinical vocabulary.

pipeline.label: LLM Agent
pipeline.description: For ambiguous, complex, or non-standard sigs, an LLM agent resolves extraction with structured prompting and constrained output schemas.

pipeline.label: Dose Normalization
pipeline.description: Extracted values are normalized to standard units (mg/day equivalent) and mapped to OMOP drug concept IDs for cross-site comparability.

pipeline.label: Validated Exposure
pipeline.description: Final structured steroid exposure records with daily dose estimates, adjudicated against gold-standard chart review for precision/recall evaluation.

demoNote: Patient is a 45-year-old female with dermatomyositis presenting for follow-up. Current immunosuppressant regimen includes prednisone 40mg daily with a plan to taper by 10mg every two weeks until reaching 10mg daily, then reassess. She is also on azathioprine 150mg daily, hydroxychloroquine 400mg daily, and omeprazole 20mg daily for GI prophylaxis. Recent creatine kinase was 320 (down from 1800 at diagnosis). Aldolase 8.5. She reports improved proximal muscle strength and decreased skin involvement over the past month. Plan: continue current prednisone taper as tolerated; recheck CK and aldolase in 4 weeks; consider reducing azathioprine if labs remain stable; patient counseled on bone protection with calcium and vitamin D supplementation while on prolonged steroid therapy.

problems.text: Dermatomyositis — improving (CK 320, down from 1800 at diagnosis)
spans: creatine kinase was 320 (down from 1800 at diagnosis)

problems.text: Proximal muscle weakness — improved on current regimen

medications.text: Prednisone 40mg daily → taper 10mg q2wks → target 10mg daily
spans: taper by 10mg every two weeks until reaching 10mg daily

assessment.text: Clinical improvement — CK trending down, muscle strength recovering

plan.text: Continue prednisone taper as tolerated; recheck labs in 4 weeks
spans: recheck CK and aldolase in 4 weeks

plan.text: Consider reducing azathioprine if labs remain stable
spans: consider reducing azathioprine if labs remain stable

plan.text: Counsel on bone protection: Ca + Vit D supplementation
spans: calcium and vitamin D supplementation while on prolonged steroid therapy

title: Disease Severity Framework
subtitle: Conceptual and computational framework for representing severity
problem: Disease severity is a central concept in clinical medicine — it drives treatment intensity, prognosis, escalation decisions, and resource allocation. Yet there is no unified conceptual or computational framework for representing severity. Existing severity scores (APACHE-II, SOFA, Child-Pugh) are domain-specific predictive instruments that have been reified into measures of disease state, often encoding hidden assumptions about what severity means.
approach: My thesis has two components: (1) a PRISMA-ScR scoping review synthesizing definitions and conceptualizations of disease severity across clinical domains, and (2) a unified conceptual framework representing severity as multilevel and multidimensional — capturing objective markers, functional impact, temporal trajectory, and patient context. The framework is being formalized as a temporal state-transition model to support computational implementation.
impact: A manuscript on the conceptual framework is in preparation for Journal of Biomedical Informatics. The framework will serve as the conceptual foundation for computational severity representations that can support clinical decision support, risk stratification, and phenotyping research. It also informs an open-source systematic review platform being developed alongside the thesis.
myRole: Primary investigator: scoping review design, literature screening, conceptual framework development, manuscript writing. PI: Dr. Harold P. Lehmann, Johns Hopkins School of Medicine.

pipeline.label: Scoping Review
pipeline.description: PRISMA-ScR protocol across PubMed, EMBASE, and clinical informatics literature — synthesizing how severity is defined, operationalized, and measured across cardiology, oncology, neurology, and other domains.

pipeline.label: Conceptual Analysis
pipeline.description: Extraction and coding of severity dimensions: biomarkers, functional status, disease trajectory, patient context, and clinical judgment. Identifying commonalities and domain-specific variation.

pipeline.label: Framework Design
pipeline.description: Developing a multilevel, multidimensional conceptual framework — representing severity as a composite construct that integrates objective, functional, temporal, and contextual dimensions.

pipeline.label: Formalization
pipeline.description: Translating the conceptual framework into a temporal state-transition model using ontology-informed structures for computational representation.

pipeline.label: Validation
pipeline.description: Validating framework coverage against clinical vignettes and existing severity scores; identifying gaps in current operationalizations.

decisionNodes.label: Biomarker Level
decisionNodes.rationale: The most common dimension: objective physiological or laboratory markers that deviate from normal. Examples: troponin in ACS, creatinine in CKD, CD4 count in HIV. Often the most measurable but not sufficient to capture severity.
decisionNodes.next: Feeds into functional impairment assessment — how biomarker deviation translates to lived impact.

decisionNodes.label: Functional Impact
decisionNodes.rationale: How the disease limits what a patient can do. NYHA class for heart failure, ECOG status for cancer, ADL/IADL for frailty. Captures severity as experienced by the patient, not just measured by clinicians.
decisionNodes.next: Both biomarker level and functional impact are shaped by disease trajectory over time.

decisionNodes.label: Temporal Trajectory
decisionNodes.rationale: Whether the disease is stable, progressing, or remitting changes the clinical meaning of any snapshot. A creatinine of 2.1 means very different things if it rose from 1.8 last week versus has been stable for 3 years.
decisionNodes.next: Trajectory interacts with patient context to determine clinical urgency and treatment response.

decisionNodes.label: Patient Context
decisionNodes.rationale: Comorbidities, age, frailty, and social context modulate how a given biomarker or functional state maps onto severity for a specific patient. The same SOFA score carries different weight in a 30-year-old vs. an 80-year-old with multiple organ dysfunction.
decisionNodes.next: Integrating all dimensions yields a severity representation that supports clinical decision-making.

decisionNodes.label: Composite Severity
decisionNodes.rationale: No single dimension captures severity fully. The framework integrates biomarker, functional, trajectory, and context dimensions into a composite representation that can support risk stratification, treatment intensity decisions, and phenotyping research.
decisionNodes.next: Enables downstream applications: CDS, phenotyping, comparative effectiveness, prognostic modeling.

title: OHDSI Network Study: Dermatomyositis Phenotyping
subtitle: Computable phenotypes and multi-site comparative effectiveness in OMOP
status: Active · Myositis Center, Johns Hopkins Rheumatology
problem: Dermatomyositis is a rare inflammatory myopathy with significant treatment variability and poorly characterized adverse event profiles. Because it is rare, single-site studies are underpowered. Multi-site studies using heterogeneous data systems are complex to coordinate. OHDSI's federated network approach offers a path to the scale needed — but only if phenotyping algorithms are carefully validated across sites.
approach: I developed computable phenotypes and cohort discovery pipelines for dermatomyositis across multi-institution OMOP CDM databases using OHDSI ATLAS, contributing to a distributed network study evaluating immunosuppressant therapies and medication-related adverse events. I'm also developing an R package to support distributed cohort evaluation and meta-analysis across OHDSI partner databases.
impact: The phenotyping approach enables a properly powered multi-site comparative effectiveness analysis that would be impossible at any single institution. The R package, once released, will support reproducible distributed cohort evaluation for rare disease research across the OHDSI community.
myRole: Developed computable phenotypes, contributed to study design and comparative effectiveness analysis. Leading R package development for distributed cohort evaluation. PI: Dr. Christopher A. Mecoli, Johns Hopkins Rheumatology.

pipeline.label: OMOP CDM Data
pipeline.description: Source: multi-institution OMOP CDM databases with condition, drug, measurement, and observation domains standardized to OMOP vocabulary.

pipeline.label: ICD Phenotyping
pipeline.description: Initial cohort defined using ICD-10 codes for dermatomyositis and related inflammatory myopathy diagnoses, filtered by code frequency and setting requirements.

pipeline.label: Cohort Diagnostics
pipeline.description: OHDSI CohortDiagnostics evaluates phenotype performance across sites: prevalence, incidence, attrition, and concept set coverage before any analysis begins.

pipeline.label: Exposure Definition
pipeline.description: Immunosuppressant exposure cohorts defined using OMOP drug concept hierarchies: corticosteroids, azathioprine, mycophenolate, rituximab, IVIG.

pipeline.label: Distributed Analysis
pipeline.description: Analysis package runs locally at each site; only aggregate results are shared. Meta-analysis synthesizes site-level estimates into network-wide comparative effectiveness results.

title: Racial Disparities in Post-Surgical ED Utilization
subtitle: Multi-state retrospective cohort study using HCUP data
status: Published (In Press) · Int J Gynaecol Obstet
problem: Racial disparities in surgical outcomes and post-operative healthcare utilization are well-documented in aggregate, but the mechanisms and modifiable factors are less understood. Emergency department visits within 30 days of hysterectomy represent a measurable, clinically meaningful outcome that reflects both quality of care and access to follow-up.
approach: Processed multi-state HCUP databases covering over 48 million inpatient and outpatient records. Designed cohort selection and filtering pipelines to identify approximately 200,000 hysterectomy cases. Built multivariable logistic regression and sensitivity analysis workflows in R to evaluate predictors of 30-day ED utilization, with race/ethnicity as the primary exposure and a broad set of clinical and demographic covariates.
impact: First-author, corresponding author publication in International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics (in-press). Results characterize systematic racial disparities in post-hysterectomy ED utilization and identify clinical factors that partially mediate and partially moderate these disparities, with implications for targeted quality improvement interventions.
myRole: First and corresponding author. Designed cohort selection pipeline, built all analytical workflows in R, generated all statistical outputs, wrote the manuscript.

pipeline.label: HCUP Databases
pipeline.description: Multi-state HCUP inpatient and outpatient databases — 48M+ records. Covers procedures, diagnoses, demographics, and payer information at the encounter level.

pipeline.label: Cohort Selection
pipeline.description: Hysterectomy cases identified by procedure codes (ICD-10-PCS / CPT). Exclusions applied for age, comorbidity extremes, and incomplete follow-up windows. Final cohort: ~200,000 cases.

pipeline.label: Outcome Ascertainment
pipeline.description: 30-day ED visits identified by linking index hospitalization to subsequent ED encounters within the same state database, using probabilistic and deterministic linkage strategies.

pipeline.label: Statistical Modeling
pipeline.description: Multivariable logistic regression adjusting for age, insurance, comorbidities, hospital characteristics, procedure type, and state. Sensitivity analyses test alternative cohort definitions.

pipeline.label: Disparity Analysis
pipeline.description: Racial/ethnic differences in ED utilization estimated as adjusted odds ratios. Mediation and interaction analyses evaluate contributions of modifiable vs. structural factors.
